macro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Framework: When a Pesach Can Be Brought as Shelamim vs. When Only Proceeds Can Be Used

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah's distinction between pushed-away (nidcheh) and available pesach animals
The case of a lost pesach: if found before shechita (slaughter), it is nidcheh; if found after shechita, it is not
Application to temurah (exchange): the temurah inherits the status of the original pesach

macro_segments.title: Rava's Teaching on Temurah: When a Temurah Can Be Brought Itself Despite the Original Being Nidcheh

micro_segments.title: Rava's distinction: temurah can only be brought if the original pesach was found after shechita
When the original is nidcheh (found before shechita) and temurah is done after shechita, the temurah remains stigmatized via inherited kedusha
Visual explanation of the timeline: when the original sheep was lost, a new one sanctified, and then slaughtered

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Challenge to Rava: Using the Pesach Pasuk to Question the Nidcheh Doctrine

micro_segments.title: Abaye questions whether the pasuk 'im kesev' (if a lamb) is necessary or redundant given it could be inferred from context
The pasuk teaches that a temurah of pesach can be brought as shelamim after pesach is brought
Abaye's logical analysis: if the simple case (found after shechita) is obvious, the pasuk must come to teach the non-obvious case
The Gemara resolves that the pasuk is merely asmachta (support), not a source of new law in this context

macro_segments.title: The True Purpose of the Pesach Pasuk: Teaching Details About Fat and Conversion to Shelamim

micro_segments.title: The pasuk teaches that when a pesach cannot be brought, it is converted into shelamim with all shelamim laws applied
The word 'im kesev' includes pesach in the fat-tail law: even a pesach (if a lamb) includes the fat tail
The word 'im' teaches that shelamim laws (smicha, nesachim, tenufah) apply to a pesach that becomes shelamim
The exclusion 've-im ez' (if a goat): goats do not have a fat tail, teaching that a goat pesach does not include fat tail

macro_segments.title: Alternative Version: Rava's Teaching Applied to the Beginning of the Mishnah (Proceeds Case)

micro_segments.title: Rava's statement reapplied: the question of whether temurah can be used depends on when the temurah act was performed
If temurah was done after shechita, it can be brought; if before shechita, it cannot, because shechita only establishes nidcheh status when it rejects a fit animal

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Second Challenge from a Different Braitta: Using 'Pesach Hu' Pasuk to Prove Temurah Cannot Be Brought

micro_segments.title: A braitta teaches that only a real pesach (pesach hu) can be brought, excluding certain temurot
Abaye's logical analysis: if temurah was always excluded, the pasuk would be obvious; so it must come to teach the non-obvious case
The non-obvious case is when the original was found before shechita but the temurah was done after shechita
The Gemara accepts this as a strong refutation of Rava's position: the pasuk teaches that temurah cannot be brought in this scenario

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Cryptic Principle: Comparing Pesach to Chatas to Determine Status

micro_segments.title: Shmuel introduces a rule: any scenario that causes a chatas to die (misa) causes a pesach to be brought as shelamim; any scenario that causes a chatas to graze (roeh) causes a pesach to graze
The five cases where a chatas dies (Vitnnah mnemonic): vlad (offspring), tmura (exchange), mesu (owner died), nickpru (atonement done elsewhere), avar shnaso (over a year old)
Not all five cases apply to pesach: only avud (lost), mesu bealeiha (owner died), and avar shnaso (over a year old) are relevant; vlad and tmura don't apply because pesach must be male

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Challenge: The Avar Shnaso Case Undermines Shmuel's Principle

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef challenges: a chatas that is over a year old (avar shnaso) belongs to Reish Lakish's position that it grazes, not dies
By pesach, avar shnaso is explicitly brought as shelamim (from the braitta on kesev), not left to graze
Therefore, Shmuel's principle does not hold universally: chatas grazes but pesach is shelamim in the same scenario

macro_segments.title: Resolving Rav Yosef's Challenge: Shmuel Speaks Only of the Avud (Lost) Scenario

micro_segments.title: The Gemara clarifies: Shmuel's principle applies only when a pesach is lost (avud), not in cases of other problems like age or owner death
The Braitta on Lost Chatas: if a chatas is designated and lost, a new one is separated, and the original is found, one is brought and one dies (Rabbi's view)
The Chakhamim's view: if found before the new one was brought, it can be redeemed (roeh); if found after the new one was brought, it dies (misa)

macro_segments.title: Applying the Lost Chatas Framework to Pesach: Before or After Shechita

micro_segments.title: If a chatas is found before the new one is brought and was pushed away, it can graze (roeh); if found after, it must die (misa)
Translating to pesach: misa means the pesach is brought as pesach; roeh means the pesach is brought only as shelamim from proceeds
This perfectly matches the before/after shechita principle that Rabbi Yochanan and the Mishnah teach: the difference is whether the original was pushed away

macro_segments.title: The Problem: Shmuel's Perfect Analogy Says the Same Thing as Rabbi Yochanan, Making Him Redundant

micro_segments.title: If Shmuel's principle simply parallels Rabbi Yochanan's before/after shechita rule, he adds nothing new
The Gemara must reinterpret Shmuel as adopting a different position: he relies on before/after chatzot (noon), not before/after shechita
Under this interpretation, a pesach found after chatzot but before shechita would be brought as shelamim (not like a chatas, which would be roeh)

macro_segments.title: Shmuel Holds Like Rabbi: All Lost Chataot Always Die (No Roeh Case)

micro_segments.title: Shmuel adopts Rabbi's position: any lost chatas dies (misa), there is no roeh case for lost chataot
Applied to pesach: any pesach lost at a relevant time is brought as shelamim, regardless of before/after shechita
This means Shmuel holds that the key is whether the pesach is considered avud (lost) at a relevant time, not the act of shechita

macro_segments.title: Defining When a Pesach Is Considered Avud: Before Chatzot Is Not Avud

micro_segments.title: A pesach lost and found before chatzot (noon) is not considered avud, just as a chatas lost at night is not considered avud
Rava teaches: a loss at night is not a loss because nighttime is irrelevant to korban service anyway
Similarly, a pesach lost and found before chatzot is not a proper avud because before chatzot is irrelevant to pesach service
Therefore, Shmuel's principle applies only to pesach lost and found after chatzot: in that case, it is always brought as shelamim

macro_segments.title: The Core Disagreement: Shmuel (Chatzot) vs. Rabbi Yochanan (Shechita)

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's entire principle aims to show that the determining factor is chatzot (time), not shechita (the act)
Rabbi Yochanan's position is the opposite: shechita determines whether a pesach is nidcheh and therefore whether only proceeds can be used
By drawing the analogy to chatas status as avud (lost at a relevant time), Shmuel implies that what matters is whether the pesach is properly avud (relevant timing), which is about chatzot

macro_segments.title: Alternative Version: Could Shmuel Hold Like Rabbi Holding Shechita Determines?

micro_segments.title: The Gemara considers whether Shmuel could hold Rabbi's position (all lost chataot die) while the pesach still goes by shechita, making a perfect chatas-pesach parallel
This would mean: chatas found before shechita grazes, pesach found before shechita uses proceeds; chatas found after shechita dies, pesach found after shechita is brought
But this makes Shmuel say exactly what Rabbi Yochanan says, which is redundant and defeats the purpose of introducing Shmuel
Therefore, this interpretation is rejected; Shmuel must hold chatzot determines, not shechita

macro_segments.title: Final Resolution: Shmuel Teaches via Chatas Analogy That Chatzot (Time), Not Shechita (Act), Is Determinative

micro_segments.title: Shmuel holds like Rabbi: all avudim (lost animals) in a chatas always die (misa)
The Gemara narrows the discussion: a pesach lost and found before chatzot does not count as avud (just like a chatas lost at night doesn't count as avud)
When a pesach is properly avud (lost at a relevant time, meaning after chatzot), it is always brought as shelamim, establishing that time (chatzot) determines status, not the act of shechita
The conclusion: Shmuel teaches that what makes a pesach nidcheh or otherwise is defined by its status as avud based on time (chatzot), not by the shechita act

Chakhamim: found before new one brought = roeh; found after = misa
